item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, aside from a couple formatting changes (not to mention potential updates to the Update Log, as 2006 will be fifteen years ago come next year), this SCP holds up pretty well in both description and idea. +1

item.title: The Obvious Pun
item.content:encoded:
This article is great for the sole reason that it allows me to make the following pun.

A relationship with a statue must be pretty… hard.

item.title: Re: Why is this not Eleven from Stranger Things?
item.content:encoded: This ain't Netflix.

item.title: Why is this not Eleven from Stranger Things?
item.content:encoded: Seriously. Why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: cheesy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always thought this skip was humorous in all the right ways along with being truly interesting as a concept.
+1
(However I do like the old photo better than the new one)

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: New image is from u/nthdayoncaprica from reddit who kindly took a picture of the same memorial as the original photo and released it under cc-by-sa 3.0 for us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
011.
If you look at that number, and use your imagination, it looks like a round bullet heading towards a pair of incredibly thin crows.
Weewoo, weewoo, controversy! This guy isn’t contained! Yeah, I can see why some people are getting mad at this. Firstly, yes, it’s going to the caretaker that convinced him to climb down. Their first name has nine letters, the second name had five. It’s heavily implied. And no, I don’t think the foundation is actually just letting them take their stony faced boyfriend home. I think the original author was trying to just say that the foundation is making them the sole and main caretaker of the skip, and moving them to a secure location. Is that fair? No. Is it mentioned in the article? No. But it’s an older scip, and as such, I’m willing to forgive it, especially since the rest is just so damn appealing. It’s a smart Idea and the rest is good execution. Do I think a few small edits could fix this article to an even higher standard? Sure. Am I going to downvote it? No.
Oh, and about the whole damn birds flashgame thing, I think that the twist at the end of this sets it appart from that enough to consider it being inspired, if it even came out second.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So cute and sweet. I enjoy immensely articles that aren't creepy, kill happy, and/or so so mysterious. It's just an adorable short article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I did some digging into this scip (because I was bored) and I believe I've found the author of 011 and 109.

Im not really sure if this is the guy though, but it is from 2008, so I guess it checks out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda wish there was an interview or something

item.title: So quaint and cute! Definite +1
item.content:encoded: This was one of the first articles I ever read on here, years ago before I had an account, and I think I love it even more now than I did then! I love how polite and nice he seems, and I love that he was able to be able to be out of containment, even if it seems silly compared to how humanoid skips are treated in the later Series. Also, I now wanna read a Tale about the statue and the person he has a crush on :D

item.title: MRI on SCP-011?
item.content:encoded: Perhaps SCP-011 is in some way correlated to SCP-1121? It seems plausible. Possible MRI scan could help understanding of SCP-011.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get that the janitor who got 011 to get down from the pedestal is the one who got custody over him, and the one who reported the "attraction", but judging by the comments it could be made much more clear…

Ah well, it's a good story with a (surprisingly) happy ending. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obviously this old-ass article is nowhere near today's standards, and I'm really just beating (fortifying?) a dead horse by upvoting this article, but I'm doing it anyway because I just like it. I don't even mind the nowadays-unthinkable ending of allowing a sentient statue leave containment in order to presumably start a normal life. It's cute god dammit.

I would even go so far as to say it makes sense in-universe if you take in to account that even a tightass organization like the Foundation is still gonna have a few low priority objects that slip through the cracks, or maybe are allowed to slip by overly-empathetic researchers.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I feel kind of sorry for the caretaker. You have a stone statue that's fallen in love with you.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
The 1st image in this article is a CC BY-NC-SA image from http://www.freefoto.com/preview/1213-03-78/Civil-war-statue-in-Woodstock--Vermont. Note that the image is used here under a different license than the rest of the site. Commercial use of the image is not permitted by its creator.


This automated message was sent on behalf of anqxyr. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact anqxyr in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was deliverred incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dont see how any of you expect the author to come back and tidy up an age old article.

It has all these upvotes because people like it, simple as that.
It got my vote cause I thought it was cute, not because it exemplifies the foundation and their containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was totally with this article until the last part where someone fell in love with it and they let it go live with them??

The rest is charming enough though that I'm not inclined to down vote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too silly. It could work, but as soon as I saw the notes at the end about it falling in love with some lady and being released, no strings attached, into said lady's custody… I downvoted. That's just not how the Foundation do.

Something's not right here. If it's an old article with outdated standards, then it should have more downvotes to warrant a rewrite. But it doesn't, it's in this weird limbo between being a viable rewriting option and being an oldie but a goodie.

I think the problem is that most of the upvotes are way ancient, from when it came out, and it's not getting any modern attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is incredibly silly. That's not a bad thing on its own, but it doesn't really have anything interesting to it, at the moment; it's just a statue what became human and used to shoot at things but doesn't anymore.

There's also the fact that, as others have mentioned, the subject is not being properly contained. Regardless of whether the population of the town is aware of its existence, or whether it has romantic feelings for its caretaker, the Foundation isn't securing, containing /or/ protecting it at present; all they're doing is watching and hoping that nothing goes wrong. This seems especially odd considering the fact that, prior to an undisclosed date in 2001, SCP-011 had attacked humans after being partially covered in fecal matter.

I understand that this probably isn't going to be changed; it's at 166, and it's really old. Just thought I'd leave my two cents at the door.

+ Last note:
- Last note:

This was written /really/ close to the release of Damn Birds. I'm not able to find an exact release date for that game, although it was re-released on Kongregate in November of the same year, but the similarities between them bother me a little. It's probably nothing, and if it isn't, mofunzone were probably the ones doing the idea-stealing, but I felt it was worth mentioning again; the last time someone brought it up, they weren't exactly coherent about it.

item.title: Re: Possible "strong AI" type storyline
item.content:encoded: I feel that once it reached a human level of sentience, it should have been upgraded to Euclid.

item.title: Re: how is it an "SCP"
item.content:encoded:
Much respect to all the great wild guesses as to why it's written that way or how the containment might maybe work

Granted, as of today, this skip is 2217 days old. It's a remnant of a time of less-stringent standards, when there weren't many consistent requirements for writing containment procedures.

item.title: how is it an "SCP"
item.content:encoded: Downvoted until such time as the Special Containment Proceedures contain containment proceedures. Otherwise, it's lovely, but it's not an SCP. Much respect to all the great wild guesses as to why it's written that way or how the containment might maybe work, but they're not in the article. And that's bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those dates are a bit odd but still a good SCP up vote
I don't think the author for this scip is around anymore, but eh. Congratulations, author!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty funny, a statue that fights back.

Shouldn't those dates be redacted? or is it unnecessary?

Upvote from me anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is deliberately dated to 2006 — that is, it is a snapshot of what this SCP's writeup would look like in that year. It doesn't address anything that may or may not have happened since that last date.

And there is no comity/committee/whatever you meant to type. They aren't supposed to be updated periodically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been seven years, has nothing of note happened with this scip in that time? That actually makes me wonder, is there any organized editing comity for old articles like this so that they may periodically be updated with new events?

item.title: Re: Possible "strong AI" type storyline
item.content:encoded:
180 is about Einstein level intelligence and it says that it is in fact growing. While it has not been so towards humans it is clearly violent, it solves its problems with violence. When you combine those things in people you get serial murderers, when you combine them in alien (in the sense that it is not human) lifeforms may get nearly anything but few of those possibilities are good.

As for the other rule you can do whatever you like, i was just suggesting a way for the story to continue to evolve; going from a cute little funny story to something unsettling.

item.title: Re: Possible "strong AI" type storyline
item.content:encoded:
A few questions about what you are suggesting-
First of all, I've taken what the article is saying about it gaining intelligence to mean that it is becoming more human, not neccessarily just more intellectual. I.E, going from being built as a human to actually being a human.
Secondly- why would having an IQ of around 180 make it 'extremely dangerous'?
Third of all, are you aware this is an old as dirt SCP and the general (although there are exceptions) rule on those is 'if it isn't broken, don't fix it'?

item.title: Possible "strong AI" type storyline
item.content:encoded: I see a few things that one could do to take this form quirky and charming to sinister. For example given what is listed in the article the statue seems to be gaining intelligence as time goes on. If it were to breach a critical level (around 180 IQ) it would become extremely dangerous. In addition it would realize in 2006 at 133 IQ that it was getting smarter and that if it got too smart it would appear dangerous which would be why it started its romantic notions toward one of the caretakers, to make it seem disarmingly quirky. I purpose that it be moved into a facility, convincing the townsfolk to tear the statue down then taking it in the night with its willing cooperation then proceeding to move forward with the "strong AI" angle on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My personal headcanon is that the person who it was transferred to is some VIP that the Foundation trusts to keep it contained and whose gratefulness is worth more than keeping the statue in a box. She's also the reason why the Foundation let Woodstock keep the statue in the first place — she just likes it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
something like really bad lolfoundation or some other egregious error that newbies will take the wrong idea from.

Even that sets a really bad precedent. If article seems bad by new standards, call for a review, but unless it's something really really trivial, or unless the author does it themselves, I don't love the idea of wanton editting of work that can clearly stand on its merit. Defeats the purpose of rating and all that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's almost certainly better not to edit an article with such a high rating, unless it's a minor edit to remove something like really bad lolfoundation or some other egregious error that newbies will take the wrong idea from.

I don't have any issues with the article as it stands, though. Maybe the 'nullification' of the containment procedure is a little iffy (they didn't nullify it, they just switched to observation), but it isn't a huge deal… I wouldn't object to someone tweaking that, though, personally.

EDIT: Ninja'ed, sort of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's at +70 something… why edit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely an old article, but I actually like it. Perhaps an edit is in order…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is so damn charming until it gets to the last few notes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get it.
The containment procedures were just dropped? The Foundation allows a sentient statue to roam free?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this until the part where we handed containment over to some guy. Why would the foundation ever do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'5.17.2005 - █████████ █████ reports that SCP-011 is romantically attracted to her.'

Good luck with that.

Upvotified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can. Double Vision - she appeared extremely smart at first, but when her ability was accounted for, she scored just under 100 IIRC

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why are all humanoid SCPs so goddamn smart?
I can't think of a time where I read about a humanoid SCP taking an IQ test and it getting lower than 110.
This statue is a qualified genius (higher than 130), and he's never done anything other than stand around and take potshots at pigeons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I call dibs! (not really)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what you're trying to say, but this reads as if the sequel to some flash game is going to attain sentience, come here, upvote this article and then upvote you.

A fascinating SCP in its own right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anyone who played the flash game it's sequel will most definitely upvote this (including me)!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
5.17.2005 - █████████ █████ reports that SCP-011 is romantically attracted to her

I can see it now.

"May I remind all staff it is considered inappropriate to reproduce with any SCP. Those caught producing offspring will immediately be disciplined." -Dr. ████

"With the death of █████████ █████ due to childbirth I am unsure what we're going to do with a small baby statue. Perhaps if we [REDACTED]." -Dr. ████

I love this bird shootin' SCP and believe it certainly belongs here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The residents do not seem to know about it, as the cleaning of the statue is performed in the dark. One guy reporting weird behavior isn't the same thing as this being the whole town's secret. If they do know about it, why? That goes against the way the Foundation acts, especially since all it would take is one person alerting the media. Continuing, the object is now apparently in the custody of someone it loves. Even though it's being monitored, this seems somewhat dangerous as the custodian might someday return the affection and perhaps smuggle it off somewhere.

Also, there is no containment facility, all they ever did was clean the place. Which makes me ask, WHY was there never a containment facility? Usually anomalous items of this nature are smuggled away under cover of darkness and replaced with the kind that don't do weird things. As this statue is capable of removing itself from its pedestal, it's clearly movable. So what's the deal here? Why is this SCP allowed to run free when all it takes is one little thing going wrong for the Foundation to have to clean up the mess and bust out the amnesiacs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the residents have just decided to not really talk about it to outsiders, and the object itself is clearly intelligent enough to stand still when it doesn't want someone to see it move. They are monitoring it regularly, and an actual containment facility would disrupt normality more than the almost-as-good watching. Presumably, they also sweep the area for surveillance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why was it freed from containment? Where is it kept now, and how many people are involved in observations? It's a good SCP and the idea of a SCP getting out of normal containment into a simpler set-up is nice, but it would be nicer to know how the Foundation is sure it won't be seen, especially since several SCPs turned out to be way more dangerous than they initially seem. Why are they risking this thing being found out, or worse, escaping them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But what came first?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-011, the video game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: seeing statues covered in bird shit is a common thing, so the idea of a statue shooting the birds before they can defecate on it is pretty funny. i think this would actually be better if that was its only schtick, and there was nothing else besides the description, but its good both ways.
